rationale: Naylor et al. (2011) is cited in core parts of the paper, including Study 2 design choices for audience-cue conditions and the conceptual discussion of how ambiguous cues are typically interpreted. The citing authors explicitly contrast their stigma-context results with the Naylor pattern for nonstigmatized consumers, arguing that ambiguity can produce threat/devaluation inferences rather than default similarity inferences. This is more than background referencing because it helps frame hypotheses, interpret findings, and position contribution. Thus the citation plays a substantive, theory-qualifying role.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Naylor et al. (2011) as a baseline account of ambiguity-driven similarity inference, then qualifies it by showing a boundary condition: when consumers manage potentially stigmatizing attributes, ambiguous audience cues can trigger threat and social devaluation inferences. It also borrows Naylor et al.'s audience-cue manipulation logic in its experimental setup.
